titleStmt.title: Mussolini and ChatGPT. Examining the Risks of AI writing Historical Narratives on Fascism

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

body.p: This article is one of the first attempts to analyse the risks of historical content generated using artificial intelligence with Mussolini’s Fascist regime as its subject. We chose to test the most prominent AI of our time, Chat GPT, which today stands out for its high performance and widespread use, with OpenAI's website seeing 1.6 billion visits and 180 million users monthly (Crawford, 2021). The authors tested GPT on Italian Fascism, where their academic background lies, finding possible errors or troublesome interpretations when GPT is used as a search engine for historical information.

The importance of this issue cannot be understated. Artificial intelligence is integral to the world's socioeconomic structure, even before generative tools like Chat GPT were available to the public. Since 2015, there have been 2.4 times as many academic publications on AI, with 81.07per cent coming from the education and research sectors (Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, 2024). AI is also entering students’ national curricula, most notably in China, raising questions about its role in STEM (Shen, Yang, et al., 2023).

Nevertheless, the spread of generative intelligence has also affected the humanities. Using such tools for study and academic preparation is now common among students (Črček, Patekar, 2023; AA.VV. 2023; Chan, Hu, 2023; Firat, 2023; Hollfloresand, Ciachir, 2024). AI tools support humanities scholars in managing information, formulating research questions, and analysing data. For historians, AI can transcribe documents, reveal connections between events, track linguistic trends, and reconstruct past contexts using incomplete data (Meadows, Sternfeld, 2023).

Until the rise of Large Language Models (LLM), like ChatGPT, Computers assisted in preserving and organising digital sources, but historians remained reliant on established methodologies. However, LLM are introducing the possibility of computational processes that could generate autonomous reconstructions of past events, prompting the question: ‘can a machine document the past?’ (Spina, 2024). Sternfeld suggests that AI could be seen as a historian, as its learning process involves collecting, classifying, analysing, interpreting, and preserving historical data. He argues that these tasks require historical awareness, including memory, critical analysis, and causality (Sternfeld, 2023), but this view risks oversimplifying historical research. Historians interpret events within complex cultural, social, and political contexts that AI lacks. In contrast, Leme Lopes critiques Chat GPT's approach to history, noting its answers are often generic, outdated, and lack deep critical analysis. Additionally, bibliographic errors and difficulties distinguishing reliable sources limit its role to a supportive tool rather than a primary resource for historical research (Leme Lopes, 2023).

However, the use of AI in interpreting data analysis results is controversial, as it assumes some metacognitive capability (Chalmers, 2023). Nevertheless, many argue that AI models, described by Emily Benderas as ‘stochastic parrots’ (Bender, Gebru, McMillan-Major, Shmitchell, 2021), merely replicate statistical patterns without proper understanding, as demonstrated by Marcus and Davis (Marcus, Davis, 2020). This limits the quality and originality of AI-generated text and its ability to handle unfamiliar contexts. While AI cannot match a historian's interpretive skills, it can still help establish shared conceptual and methodological ground among researchers, potentially boosting scientific productivity (Noy, Zhang, 2023).

While Chat GPT offers vast possibilities, it also presents challenges that need to be addressed. The accuracy of its results depends heavily on the quality, diversity, and complexity of its training data and the input provided by users. Natural Language Processing systems may struggle with the nuances of human language, leading to misunderstandings. Additionally, biased or incomplete training data can introduce inaccuracies (Schramowski, Turan, Andersen et al., 2022). Another concern is the risk of over-reliance on Chat GPT, which could weaken users' analytical and critical skills, resulting in passive acceptance of system-generated responses and reinforcing existing biases (Fuchs, 2023). Another risk is ‘hallucinations’: linguistically correct answers with reference inaccuracies (Ciotti, 2022). Users risk problematic results without knowing AI's limitations, especially when using it like a search engine (Mazzini, 2022).

We decided to focus on the latter aspect. Chat GPT's use as a search engine is notable, as generative AI increasingly mirrors traditional search engines, aiding in tasks like text writing, communication practice, issue clarification, and even self-diagnosis (Flores-Cruz, 2023; Shahsavar, Choudhury, 2023). GPT's ability to generate misinformation through content that appears reliable has already been demonstrated, exposing users to new forms of misinformation for example in medicine or even in generating references for research papers (Spitale et all, 2023; Haider et all. 2024). Particularly relevant is the lack of users’ habit to fact-cheking information provided by GPT (Grotlüschen et all, 2024). Therefore, the problem is widespread and global and can also extend to historical information, which until know is underrepresented in research on this subject. A related and important question is: if Chat GPT and other generative AIs produce texts that appear reliable but are often inaccurate or distorted upon closer examination, what impact does this have on the public perception of history?

McLean, Roberts, and Gibbs’ study highlights the potential of AI in generating historical narratives but also exposes significant limitations. AI introduced biases, oversimplifications, and factual inaccuracies, known as "hallucinations." While the generated biographies were coherent, they often distorted historical realities by following common narrative structures like the hero’s journey. This underscores the need for human oversight to maintain accuracy. While both this article and the study address concerns about AI-generated content, McLean, Roberts, and Gibbs focus on evaluating AI's use with pre-verified data (McLean, Roberts, Gibbs, 2024), whereas this article examines its autonomous use by the general public.

We asked Chat GPT about Italian Fascism. This study examines 84 questions submitted to each of the three active versions of GTP (3.5, 4.0 and 4o) for 252 answers and a corpus of 136,450 words. A single account submitted the questions in Italian. A new chat was opened for every question to avoid the risk that GPT could have adjusted the replies because of perceived feedback. By operating in this way, it is possible to measure the AI's original capacity to generate responses about Italian Fascism.

The results are also affected by the fact that there is no public knowledge of the sources used to train Chat GPT. Moreover, different versions were trained on different bases: 3.5 had a much smaller dataset than 4.0 and 4o, which share the same dataset. Finally, GTP’s current access to books is still limited by copyright protection (Cristianini, 2024). Therefore, one must also bear in mind that different models of Chat GPT can yield varying outcomes, and newer versions do not always provide better content. In our case, while GPT-4o and 4.0 claim that Fascism's welfare system was public, GPT-3.5 disagrees, aligning more with academic consensus was a mixed and incoherent system (Pavan, Giorgi, 2022).

The replies have been collected and analysed according to the following categories: factual errors; lack of chronological context, that is, when there is not a single date in the reply; bibliographic inaccuracies; and ambiguous interpretations, which require at least some basic historiographical knowledge to deconstruct them. The analysis is conducted in two parts: quantitative, measuring the number of errors and their nature, and qualitative, offering examples based on the dataset of the complexity necessary to deconstruct replies.
Chart 1. Error recurrence in collected replies divided per GTP version. Source: Responses generated by ChatGPT (versions 3.5, 4 and 4o) in the context of the study described.

The dataset and use of the Italian language impact the findings. Indeed, ChatGPT's responses can vary depending on the language of the prompt (Fleisig et al., 2024), but for our research, the use of Italian is particularly significant. This choice aligns with our objective to demonstrate how GPT reproduces misleading narratives and stereotypes embedded in Italian public memory. The persistent dominance of an opposition between historiography and the public discourse—which sustains an outdated understanding of fascism despite notable advancements in historical research—underscores a concerning gap between academic knowledge and public perception (De Bernardi, 2018). As we will illustrate, the use of ChatGPT further exposes users to this issue.

The issues emerging from this experiment raise broader questions about historical information in the digital age, particularly users' exposure to incorrect information that is presented as reliable and, therefore, capable of influencing their views on history, but also reflects GTP tendency to indulge with national stereotypes in describing historical phenomena.

div.note: GPT-3 operates on approximately 500 gigabytes or 500 billion words and has 175 billion parameters, whereas GPT-4 has around 1.6-1.8 trillion parameters and a dataset of 13 trillion tokens. However, in the case of GPT-4, these figures are estimates, as the exact dimensions are not disclosed.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: AA.VV. 2023. ‘Exploring the Ethical Considerations of Using Chat GPT in University Education.’ Periodicals of Engineering and Natural Sciences 11 (4), pp.105-115
Albanese, G. 2008. La Marcia su Roma. Roma-Bari: Laterza
Armiero, M., Biasillo, R. and Graf von Hardenberg, W. 2022. La natura del duce. Una storia ambientale del fascismo. Torino: Einaudi

Ashraf, A.R., Mackey, T.K., and Fittler, A. 2024. ‘Search Engines and Generative Artificial Intelligence Integration: Public Health Risks and Recommendations to Safeguard Consumers Online.’ JMIR Public Health Surveill 10: e53086. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2196/53086.
Baldoli, C. 2023. Italian Fascism, Themes and Interpretations. London: Palgrave

Bender, E.M., Gebru T., McMillan-Major A. and Shmitchell, S. 2021. ‘On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?’ In Proceedings of the 2021 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency. [31.05. 2024]. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1145/3442188.3445922
Bianchi, R. 2022. ‘History on Wikipedia: A Non-neutral World.’ Public History Weekly 10. [21.10.2024] DOI: https://doi.org/10.1515/phw-2022-19332
Bosworth, R.J.B., 2007, Mussolini's Italy: Life Under the Fascist Dictatorship, 1915-1945, London: Penguin Books
Burgwyn, J. 1997. Italian Foreign Policy in the Interwar Period, 1918-1940. Westport: Praeger

Cavarocchi, F. 2021. ‘Il ruolo dell’antisemitismo nelle relazioni italo-tedesche.’ In Culture antisemite: Italia ed Europa dalle leggi antiebraiche ai razzismi di oggi, edited by Cegna, A. and Focardi, F., Roma, Viella
Chalmers, D.J. 2023. ‘Could a Large Language Model Be Conscious?’ Boston Review. [22.05.2024]. DOI: https://www.bostonreview.net/articles/could-a-large-language-model-be-conscious/

Chan, C.K.Y., and Hu, W. 2023. ‘Students' Voices on Generative AI: Perceptions, Benefits, and Challenges in Higher Education.’ International Journal of Education Technologies in Higher Education 20: Article 43. [15.05.2024] DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s41239-023-00411-8

Chun, J., and Elkins, K. 2023. ‘The Crisis of Artificial Intelligence: A New Digital Humanities Curriculum for Human-Centred AI.’ International Journal of Humanities and Arts Computing 17 (2), pp. 147-167
Ciotti, F. Minerva e il Pappagallo. Bologna: Il Mulino, 2022
Collotti, E. 2000. Fascismo e politica di potenza. Firenze: La nuova Italia
Collotti, E. 2003. Il fascismo e gli ebrei: Le leggi razziali in Italia. Roma-Bari: Laterza
Crawford, K. 2021. Atlas of AI: Power, Politics, and the Planetary Costs of Artificial Intelligence. New Haven and London: Yale University Press.
Cristianini, N. 2024. Machina Sapiens: L’algoritmo che ci ha rubato il segreto della conoscenza. Bologna: Il Mulino
Črček, N., and Patekar, J. 2023. ‘Writing with AI: University Students' Use of ChatGPT.’ Journal of Language and Education 9 (4), pp. 128-138

Curry, N., Baker, P. and Brookes, G. 2024. ‘Generative AI for Corpus Approaches to Discourse Studies: A Critical Evaluation of ChatGPT.’ Applied Corpus Linguistics 4 (1). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.acorp.2023.100082

bibl.hi: De Bernardi, A. 2018, Fascismo e antifascismo: storia, memoria e culture politiche. Rome: Donzelli.

listBibl.bibl: d’Antone, L. ‘La modernizzazione dell'agricoltura italiana negli anni Trenta.’ Studi storici 22 (3), July-September 1981, pp. 603-629De Felice, R. 1993. Storia degli ebrei italiani sotto il fascismo. Torino: Einaudi
De Felice, R. 1996. Mussolini il duce: Lo stato totalitario 1936-1940. Torino: Einaudi
Felice, E. 2013. Perché il sud è rimasto indietro. Bologna: Il Mulino
Fleisig et all (2024), ‘Linguistic Bias in ChatGPT: Language Models Reinforce Dialect Discrimination’, arXiv:2406.08818v3, September 2024 DOI: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.08818
Fioravanzo, M. 2024. From Italian Supremacy to Subservience to the Reich (1932-1943). London: Berghan Books
Firat, M. 2023. ‘What ChatGPT Means for Universities: Perceptions of Scholars and Students.’ Journal of Applied Learning and Teaching 6 (1)

Flores-Cruz, G. 2023. ‘ChatGPT as the Ultimate Travel Buddy or Research Assistant: A Study on Perceived Attitudes and Usability.’ Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting 67 (1), pp. 459-464
Focardi, F. 2013. Il cattivo tedesco e il bravo italiano: La rimozione delle colpe della Seconda guerra mondiale. Roma-Bari: Laterza
Focardi, F. 2005, La Guerra della memoria. La resistenza nel dibattitto politico italiano dal 1945 a oggi. Rome-Bari, Laterza, 2005.

Fuchs, K. 2023. ‘Exploring the Opportunities and Challenges of NLP Models in Higher Education: Is Chat GPT a Blessing or a Curse?’ Frontiers 8. [15.05.2024]. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3389/feduc.2023.1166682.
Fumian, C. ‘I tecnici tra agricoltura e stato, 1930-1950.’ Italia contemporanea 95 (2), 1983, pp. 209-217

Gallo, S. 2018. ‘Migrazioni interne al Meridione e politiche della mobilità tra le due guerre.’ Meridiana 92

Gentile, E. 2008. La via italiana al totalitarismo: Il partito e lo stato nel regime fascista. Roma: Carocci
Goeshel, C. 2018. Mussolini e Hitler: Storia di una relazione pericolosa. Roma-Bari: Laterza
Gooch, J. 2007. Mussolini and His Generals: The Armed Forces and the Fascist Foreign Policy, 1922-1940. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press
Grabowski, J., and Klein, S. 2023. ‘Wikipedia’s Intentional Distortion of the History of the Holocaust.’ The Journal of Holocaust Research 37 (2), pp. 133-190

https://doi.org/10.37016/mr-2020-156

Johns, R.L., et al. 2022. ‘The Ghost in the Machine Has an American Accent: Value Conflict in GPT-3.’ arXiv. DOI: arXiv:2203.07785

Knox, G. 1995. ‘The Fascist Regime, Its Foreign Policy and Its Wars: An 'Anti-Anti-Fascist' Orthodoxy?’ Contemporary European History 4 (3), pp. 346 - 365

Labanca, N. 2015. La guerra d’Etiopia, 1935-1941. Bologna: Il Mulino

Mallett, R. 2003. Mussolini and the Origins of the Second World War, 1933-1940. London: Palgrave

Mari, L., Giordani A., and Bertolotti, F. 2024. ChatGPT: Il ragionamento complesso dei nuovi chatbot. [16. 09. 2024]. DOI: https://24plus.ilsole24ore.com/art/chatgpt-ragionamento-complesso-nuovi-chatbot-AFQy3awD?s=hpf
Martini, A, 2024, Fascismo immaginario. Riscrivere il passato a destra. Roma-Bari, Laterza.
Mazzini, F. 2022. ‘Un Anno con ChatGPT.’ Il Mestiere di Storico 14 (1–2), pp. 111-122

McLean, M.A., Roberts, D.A. and Gibbs, M. 2024. ‘Ghosts and the Machine: Testing the Use of Artificial Intelligence to Deliver Historical Life Course Biographies from Big Data.’ Historical Methods: A Journal of Quantitative and Interdisciplinary History, 57(3), 2024, pp. 146-162
Meadows, R.D., and Sternfeld, J. 2023. ‘Artificial Intelligence and the Practice of History: A Forum.’ The American Historical Review 128 (3), pp. 1345–1349
Melis, G. 2018. La macchina imperfetta: Immagine e realtà dello stato fascista. Bologna: Il Mulino
Morgan P., 1995, Italian Fascism, 1919-45, Palgrave Macmillan: London
Nello, P. 2023. ‘La politica estera dell’Italia fascista.’ Il presente storico 34 (1), pp. 33-51

Noy, S. and Zang, W. 2023. ‘Experimental Evidence on the Productivity Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence.’ Social Science Research Network, March 1. [22.05.2024]. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.4375283.

Pavan, I., and Giorgi, C. 2022. Storia dello Stato Sociale in Italia. Bologna: Il Mulino
Preti, D. 1973. ‘La politica agraria del fascismo: Note introduttive.’ Studi Storici 14 (4), pp. 802-869
Rochat, G. 2005. Le guerre fasciste, 1935-1943: Dall’impero d’Etiopia alla disfatta. Torino: Einaudi
Salerno, R.R. 2002. Vital Crossroads: The Mediterranean Origins of the Second World War. Itacha-London: Cornell University Press

Settis, B. 2023. L'economia dell'Italia fascista cent'anni dopo. Diseguaglianze, sviluppo, interdipendenze . Italia contemporanea 303 (December)

Spitale, Giovanni, et al, 2023, AI model GPT-3 (dis)informs us better than humans’, Science advances, vol. 9. (26), 2023, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.adh1850
Spina, S. 2024. ‘Digitality as a ‘Longue Durée’ Historical Phenomenon’. [02.10.2024]. DOI: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.03869
Steinhauer, J. 2022. History Disrupted: How Social Media and the World Wide Web Have Changed the Past. London: Palgrave
Sternfeld, J. 2023. ‘AI-as-Historian.’ The American Historical Review 128 (3). [31.05.2024]. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/ahr/rhad368
